section.para: This document is an example of how you can use MobilityDB in order to analyse data. It is directly inspired by the MobilityDB Workshop already existing. We will throughout this document present different use cases of MobilityDB, which is an extension on PostGIS and PostgreSQL. Later on, we will also introduce new modules that will be presented in time.

This workshop of sorts was made by two students from Rennes during an internship. We will, for our examples, use data from STAR which is the Rennes' public transport company. This data is protected by ODbL (Open Database License)\cite{SourceSTAR}.
While this workshop illustrates the usage of MobilityDB functions, it does not explain them in detail. If you need help concerning the functions of MobilityDB, please refer to the documentation.

section.title: Installing MobilityDB
section.para:
For this workshop, you will need to have MobilityDB installed. Here is a little guide to do that.
If you run Ubuntu 22.04 (Jammy Jellyfish), you can simply execute the following in a terminal:
sudo apt update sudo apt # We will need the foloowing packages: sudo apt install curl ca-certificates gnupg
# Install the public key for the repository (if not done previously): curl https://www.postgresql.org/media/keys/ACCC4CF8.asc | gpg --dearmor | sudo tee /etc/apt/trusted.gpg.d/apt.postgresql.org.gpg >/dev/null
# Create the repository configuration file: sudo sh -c 'echo "deb http://apt.postgresql.org/pub/repos/apt/ jammy-pgdg main" >> /etc/apt/sources.list.d/postgresql.list' sudo apt update sudo apt upgrade
# Install the mobilitydb package with apt. It will automatically install the dependancies needed, PosgreSQL and PostGIS in particular. sudo apt install postgresql-16-mobilitydb

section.title: PgAdmin4: a PostgreSQL Editor
section.para: We are going to execute SQL commands. To do so, you can do it in a terminal or use any editor you like. If you do not have one, here is a guide to install PgAdmin4, which is the one we used: https://www.pgadmin.org/download/pgadmin-4-apt/
You could also download DataGrip from Jetbrains. We didn’t use it in this workshop, but we’re sure it’s a good tool.

section.title: Starting the database
section.para:
In order to start the PostgreSQL database, you need to do as following, replacing username by your own username.
sudo -u postgres createuser -s -i -d -r -l -w <username> sudo -u postgres createdb workshop sudo service postgresql start psql -U <username> -d workshop

section.title: Importing the data
section.para: You can find in the repository for this workshop a CSV file called Freq_Mars2024 data.csv. It should look like the Figure 1.

figure.title: First few lines of the table

section.para: This is the data we will use for this workshop. If you want more recent data, you can find it here by selecting the month you want.

The columns in the table are listed below, with in the first column af the table the original name (in french), in the second column the english translation and in the third column an explanation of the column:

row.entry: Beginning of the 15 minutes time slot that encompasses the time of that stop, format: 23:59:59
id of the stop for that entry
Name of the town that stop is in
id of the line for that entry
Human readable name of that line
Direction the bus is going in, 'indéterminé' if it’s not a bus
An estimation of the real number of people that got on the bus at that stop, based on the number of recorded people and indicators like the fraud rate.

section.para:
Then, we need to create a table in the database to store this data. Here is how to create the first three columns of the table:
CREATE TABLE RennesInput( DateFreq date, TimeSlot15mn time, Timeo integer, We let you create the rest of the table. See you next page to see the full table!

Here is how to create the full table:
CREATE TABLE RennesInput( DateFreq date, TimeSlot15mn time, Timeo integer, StopName varchar(50), TownName varchar(50), LineID integer, LineName varchar(50), Direction varchar(20), NbPersBoarding varchar(50) );

section.title: Introduction
section.para: In this chapter, we are going to use GTFS data. GTFS (General Transit Feed Specification) is a standard format for public transportation schedules and associated geographic information. It is used by many public transportation companies to share their data.

We are going to use the GTFS data from STAR, the public transportation company of Rennes. You can find the data here, but it will give you the updated data. The exact data we are using in this workshop can be found in the repo, in the GTFS folder.
We are not going to use the xxx and xxx files because it is not relevant for our workshop.

section.title: Importing the data
section.para:
Now, we need to import the GTFS data into the database, you need to create a table for each file. Here is how to create the table for the agengency.txt file:
CREATE TABLE agency ( agency_id text DEFAULT '', agency_name text DEFAULT NULL, agency_url text DEFAULT NULL, agency_timezone text DEFAULT NULL, agency_lang text DEFAULT NULL, agency_phone text DEFAULT NULL, agency_fare_url text DEFAULT NULL, agency_email text DEFAULT NULL, CONSTRAINT agency_pkey PRIMARY KEY (agency_id) );
We let you create the rest of the tables. See you next page to see all the tables!

This might seem barbaric at first sight, but don't worry, we are going to explain everything. The "DROP TABLE IF EXISTS" command is used to delete the table if it already exists. This is useful if you want to recreate the table. For each table, we created as many columns as there are in the file. We also created a primary key for each table. For some of them, we created an index to speed up the queries. We also created exception_types, route_types, location_types and pickup_dropoff_types tables. These tables are used to store the description of the types used in the other tables, so that it would be easier to understand instead of 0, 1 and 2.

In these tables, we need to insert the description of the types. Here is how to do it for the table exception_types:
INSERT INTO exception_types (exception_type, description) VALUES (1, 'service has been added'), (2, 'service has been removed'); We let you insert the description of the types for the other tables. Remember that you can find the description of the types in the GTFS reference.

So now we have the exception_types, location_types, pickup_dropoff_types, and route_types tables filled with the description of the types. We can now insert the data from the GTFS files into the tables. To do so, you need to use the COPY command. Here is how to do it for the agency.txt file:
COPY agency(agency_id,agency_name,agency_url,agency_timezone,agency_lang,agency_phone, agency_fare_url, agency_email) FROM '/path/to/agency.txt' DELIMITER ',' CSV HEADER; Of course, you need to replace '/path/to/agency.txt' by the path to the file on your computer. This SQL command could lead to an error like this:
ERROR: could not open file "/path/to/states_2020-06-01-00.csv" for reading: Permission denied HINT: COPY FROM instructs the PostgreSQL server process to read a file. You may want a client-side facility such as psql's \copy. If you encounter this error, you can use the \copy command in psql. Here is how to do it:
psql -d <database name> -c "\copy agency(agency_id,agency_name,agency_url,agency_timezone,agency_lang,agency_phone, agency_fare_url, agency_email) FROM '/path/to/agency.txt DELIMITER ',' CSV HEADER;" Once more, you need to replace '/path/to/agency.txt' by the path to the file on your computer, and <database name> by the name of your database.
We let you insert the data for the other tables. See you next page to see all the tables filled with data!
